coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesized citric acid-modified bimetallic PtNi hollow nanospheres as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of human serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Pramod K.
Son, Seong Eun
Seong, Gi Hun
coredata.dc:description: The combination of Pt with low-cost transition metal is an effective way to diminish the bulk utilization of costly Pt and to design new nanostructured materials with improved enzyme-like activity. In the present work, citric acid-functionalized platinum–nickel hollow nanospheres (CA@PtNi hNS) were synthesized through a simple one-pot wet chemical method, which involves the galvanic replacement reaction between the Ni nanoparticles and the Pt precursor that leads to the formation of hollow nanostructures. Transmission electron spectroscopic images revealed the uniformity of the CA@PtNi hNS, with an average diameter of 10.3 ± 2 nm. Moreover, zeta potential, FTIR, and XPS measurements confirmed the existence of citric acid in the CA@PtNi hNS. During synthesis, the use of citric acid not only facilitates monodispersity but also provides a negative surface charge (−11 mV) to the CA@PtNi hNS that electrostatically attracts the 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate. As-prepared CA@PtNi hNS possessed excellent peroxidase-like activity due to rich Pt surfaces, large surface area, and heterogeneous interaction between Pt and Ni atoms. Furthermore, a nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NLISA) for human serum albumin (HSA) detection was developed by replacing the enzyme in a standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay with CA@PtNi hNS. The CA@PtNi hNS based-NLISA showed sensitive detection of HSA concentrations ranging from 0 to 400 ng mL−1 with a LOD of 0.19 ng mL−1 and an average of 112% recovery of HSA from the spiked human plasma samples. The outcomes of the present study confirm the applicability of CA@PtNi hNS as substitutes for natural enzymes.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesized citric acid-modified bimetallic PtNi hollow nanospheres as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of human serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionano Engineering, Hanyang University, Ansan 426-791, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionano Engineering, Hanyang University, Ansan 426-791, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of Pt with low-cost transition metal is an effective way to diminish the bulk utilization of costly Pt and to design new nanostructured materials with improved enzyme-like activity. In the present work, citric acid-functionalized platinum–nickel hollow nanospheres (CA@PtNi hNS) were synthesized through a simple one-pot wet chemical method, which involves the galvanic replacement reaction between the Ni nanoparticles and the Pt precursor that leads to the formation of hollow nanostructures. Transmission electron spectroscopic images revealed the uniformity of the CA@PtNi hNS, with an average diameter of 10.3 ± 2 nm. Moreover, zeta potential, FTIR, and XPS measurements confirmed the existence of citric acid in the CA@PtNi hNS. During synthesis, the use of citric acid not only facilitates monodispersity but also provides a negative surface charge (−11 mV) to the CA@PtNi hNS that electrostatically attracts the 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate. As-prepared CA@PtNi hNS possessed excellent peroxidase-like activity due to rich Pt surfaces, large surface area, and heterogeneous interaction between Pt and Ni atoms. Furthermore, a nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NLISA) for human serum albumin (HSA) detection was developed by replacing the enzyme in a standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay with CA@PtNi hNS. The CA@PtNi hNS based-NLISA showed sensitive detection of HSA concentrations ranging from 0 to 400 ng mL−1 with a LOD of 0.19 ng mL−1 and an average of 112% recovery of HSA from the spiked human plasma samples. The outcomes of the present study confirm the applicability of CA@PtNi hNS as substitutes for natural enzymes.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanozymes have garnered much consideration as alternatives to natural enzymes due to their advantages over natural enzymes, including simple preparation, easy storage, high stability against harsh environmental conditions, and cost-effectiveness [1,2]. Since the realization that magnetite nanoparticles could function as nanozyme [3], a range of nanomaterials, including metal oxides, noble metals, carbon nanomaterials, and their nanocomposites, have been found to possess enzyme-like characteristics [4,5]. Of these nanomaterials, noble metals (Au, Ag, Pt, etc.) have attracted enormous attention due to their high chemical stability, well-defined synthesis methods, amenability to surface modification, good biocompatibility, high catalytic activity, and multi-enzyme mimetic activity [6–8].

Among the noble metals, Platinum (Pt) exhibits excellent intrinsic enzyme-like activities and is regarded as one of the excellent catalysts in the modern industry; Pt is currently utilized in biomolecule detection, immunoassays, and other applications. Although Pt nanostructures possess dynamic enzyme-mimicking properties, the significant expense of Pt and its constrained worldwide supply necessitates moderation of the direct use of Pt in commercial applications. Therefore, the facile production of low-cost Pt-based nanostructures with high activity and stability remains a challenge to the wide-spread commercialization of Pt nanostructures. One noteworthy strategy to address this issue is to combine Pt with a low-cost transition metal in nanoscale materials that can viably diminish the bulk utilization of costly Pt. In addition, heterogeneous nanostructures based on the combination of Pt and a transition metal show unique structural, chemical, and electronic properties distinct from the individual constituent metals [9]. Therefore, this strategy offers an opportunity to realize new catalysts with improved activity, stability, functionality, and cost-efficiency. The catalytic characteristics of nanomaterials rely upon their morphology and size, which fundamentally impact surface effectiveness and catalyst stability [2,5]. As a result, special attention has been devoted to the synthesis of Pt-transition metal-based multi-metallic nanostructures, such as nanowires [10], nanoicosahedrals [11], nanocages [12], nanocubes [13], nanobowls [14], nanooctahedrals [15], nanoframes [16], and hollow nanostructures [17], to enhance catalytic activity. Among the nanostructures as mentioned above, hollow nanostructures have drawn huge attention as potential catalysts because they exhibit large surface areas and much lower densities than solid forms with similar size and composition [18]. In addition, hollow nanostructures possess plentiful mass diffusion pathways, high stability, and low coefficients of thermal expansion, which are properties that fundamentally aid in their utilization as active catalysts [18]. Due to these characteristics, it would be attractive to produce Pt-transition metal-based bimetallic hollow nanostructures with enhanced catalytic characteristics. In this scenario, nickel (Ni), a 3d transition metal, is an advantageous option for fabricating Pt-based hollow nanostructures as it exhibits high stability, catalytic activity, redox activity, and lower standard reduction potential (−0.25 V) [19,20]. It is approached that the incorporation of Ni with Pt will not only reduce the utilization cost of Pt, but Ni will advantageously change the shape, size, and electronic structure of Pt to work as an exceptionally productive enzyme mimetic. As far as we could possibly know, the peroxidase-like activity of PtNi alloy based nanomaterials is not yet studied.

Here, we synthesized citric acid (CA) functionalized PtNi hollow nanospheres (CA@PtNi hNS) using a simple one-pot chemical method involving a galvanic replacement reaction. The galvanic replacement method offers numerous advantages, including simple reaction steps, quick reaction, use of aqueous as solvent, and synthesis of bimetallic hollow nanostructures with thin walls. As-synthesized CA@PtNi hNS with rich Pt surfaces revealed improved peroxidase-like activity for catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Furthermore, CA@PtNi hNS was successfully utilized to perform a nanozyme-linked immunosorbent assay (NLISA) to demonstrate its high signal amplification and stability as compared to the standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for human serum albumin detection [21]. Our results confirmed the potential of CA@PtNi hNS as a substitute for natural enzymes.

Precursors including nickel chloride (NiCl2), Citric acid (C6H8O5), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), phthalate buffer (composed of potassium hydrogen phthalate and sodium azide, pH 2–6), and phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) were acquired from Samjeon Chemical (Seoul, South Korea), Samchun Chemicals (Seoul, South Korea), and Polysciences (Warrington, PA, USA), respectively. The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate and human plasma were also purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The ELISA kit for human serum albumin (HSA) and unmodified detection antibody for human serum albumin (anti-HSA) were obtained from MyBioSource (San Diego, CA, USA) and LifeSpan BioSciences (Seattle, WA, USA), respectively.

A facile one-pot wet chemical method was employed to synthesize CA@PtNi hNS. Briefly, 20 mg of NiCl2 and 10 mg of CA were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water. Then, NaBH4 (20 mg) powder was quickly added to the solution under continuous stirring at 500 RPM. At this point, the solution color changes to black due to the production of intermediate NiB compounds [Fig. S1]. After 15 min., a separately prepared 2 mM H2PtCl6 solution (20 mL) was added dropwise. This step initiates the galvanic replacement reaction (GRR), in which Pt salts slowly consume the Ni nanoparticles because the standard reduction potential of Ni is lower than that of the Pt precursor [22]. After waiting for 3 h for reaction completion, the resulting black-colored solution was centrifuged (12,000 RPM, 15 min) several times and washed with water-ethanol (1:1) to neutralize the solution pH and remove side products. The use of the equal volumetric ratio of water and ethanol decreases water activity to 84%, which reduces the colloidal stability of CA@PtNi hNS solution and makes them easily separable [23]. Finally, the synthesized CA@PtNi hNS were dried at 60 °C overnight. During the synthesis, CA was used as a stabilizing agent and a functionalizing agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ni and PtNi nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison studies, bare Ni and PtNi nanostructures were synthesized using the above procedure with small modifications. For synthesizing Ni nanoparticles, 20 mg of NiCl2 was vigorously dissolved in 100 mL deionized water. Then, NaBH4 (20 mg) powder was quickly added to the solution under continuous stirring and left 3 h to complete the reaction. The final product was centrifugally washed with water and ethanol to remove side products. In the case of PtNi nanostructures, 20 mg of NiCl2 dissolved in 100 mL deionized water was reduced by NaBH4 (20 mg) powder under the continuous stirring at 500 rpm. At this point, the formation of the NiB compound takes place that can be observed by a change in solution color to black. After 15 min., an individually prepared 2 mM H2PtCl6 solution (20 mL) was added dropwise. After waiting for 3 h for reaction completion, the resulting black-colored solution was centrifuged several times and washed with water and ethanol.

Reaction parameters, such as pH conditions, temperature, concentration, incubation time, and stability were investigated using 1 mL of phthalate buffer solution containing 2 mM H2O2, 0.5 mM TMB, and 20 μg of CA@PtNi hNS. The steady-state kinetics of CA@PtNi hNS for oxidizing TMB and H2O2 substrates were investigated by varying one substrate concentration while keeping the other substrate concentration constant. During measurements, time-dependent absorption of the reaction solution was recorded at a wavelength of 652 nm. Similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), CA@PtNi hNS catalyze the oxidation of TMB substrate in the presence of H2O2 and deliver a blue-colored product with the maximal absorbance at 652 nm (ɛ = 39,000 M−1 cm−1). The kinetics parameters of the Michaelis constant (K
m ) and maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) were evaluated using Lineweaver–Burk plots of the double reciprocal of Michaelis–Menten equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of anti-HSA-CA@PtNi hNS conjugate and spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Unmodified detection antibodies specific to human serum albumin (anti-HSA) were conjugated with CA@PtNi hNS. First, 30 μL of CA@PtNi hNS aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) were well-dispersed in 960 μL of PBS. Subsequently, 10 μL of anti-HSA (6.5 mg mL−1) was added to the above solution, and the solution was shaken overnight in an icebox followed by washing with PBS. After washing anti-HSA-CA@PtNi hNS conjugates were redispersed in PBS and kept at 4 °C until further use. The FTIR characterization and change in peroxidase-like activity were studied to confirm the conjugation of CA@PtNi hNS with anti-HSA. Different concentrations of HSA ranging from 0 to 400 ng mL−1 were prepared in PBS. Whereas, the specific concentrations (50 and 200 ng mL−1) of spiked HSA samples were prepared by the addition of HSA standard antigen in human plasma solution.

NLISA was developed by replacing the enzyme in an ELISA kit for human serum albumin (HSA) with CA@PtNi hNS. A standard procedure with some minor modifications is followed during the NLISA to detect HSA. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature, and after each step, each used well was washed 3-times with 250 μL of 1× washing buffer. In the first step, the columns of 96-well plate pre-coated with capture anti-HSA antibody are treated with different concentrations of HSA (0–400 ng mL−1) and incubated overnight at 4 °C. In the second step, 100 μL of anti-HSA-CA@PtNi hNS conjugate was filled in each well and incubated for 1 h on an orbital shaker. In the final step, 100 μL of the standard working solution was added to each well, which instantly developed a blue color. After 2 min., the reaction was stopped by using 100 μL of stop solution. The resultant yellow color solution was transferred to blank wells (uncoated), and the absorbance of each well was recorded at 450 nm. A standard ELISA was also performed for comparison of results. Each detection experiments were carried out in duplicate sets under the same conditions to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of results.

The structure and morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials were investigated using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/MAX-2500, Rigaku, USA) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2010, USA), respectively. The functionalization of PtNi hNS with CA was confirmed using the zeta potential (zeta-potential analyzer, Malvern instrument ltd., UK), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo fisher scientific, USA), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Scienta R3000). Time-reaction absorbance spectra were recorded on a UV–visible spectrometer (UV–vis, OPTIZEN 2120UV, Mecasys Co. Ltd., South Korea). A multimode microplate reader (BioTek instrument Inc., USA) was used to measure absorbance at a wavelength of 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism behind the formation of CA@PtNi hNS
ce:section.ce:para: The GRR technique used herein allows for the synthesis of CA@PtNi hNS with fast reaction time and favorable conditions such as the utilization of water as solvents and straightforward reaction steps. The GRR involves redox reactions, and it critically depends on the standard reduction potential of the sacrificial metal substrate and metal ions present in solution. In this experiment, the standard reduction potential of the sacrificial Ni substrate is −0.25 V, while that of the Pt ions in solution is 0.68 V. As shown in the schematic diagram [Scheme 1 ], the addition of NaBH4 in the NiCl2 – CA solution results in the reduction of Ni2+ ions and the formation of CA-functionalized amorphous NiB compounds [24]. The addition of a Pt precursor (H2PtCl6) immediately results in galvanic replacement because of Pt’s high standard reduction potential compared to Ni. During the reaction, Pt ions reduce in metallic Pt particles and deposit onto the surface of the Ni metal, as the core NPs corrode. In this way, NiB compounds act as a sacrificial substrate and allow for the formation of hollow PtNi nanospheres.

The crystalline structure of the prepared PtNi hNS and CA@PtNi hNS was investigated using XRD (Fig. 1 ). XRD spectra of the PtNi hNS do not demonstrate any isolated diffraction peaks corresponding to Ni (JCPDS 04-0850) or Pt (JCPDS 04-0802); instead, the predominant diffraction peaks appeared on the higher angle (2θ) side of pure Pt peaks. This red-shift of the peaks compared to those of pure Pt suggests the formation of PtNi alloy without the formation of individual metallic nanoparticles [19,25]. In the case of CA@PtNi hNS, the presence of citric acid leads to the lower crystallinity that reduces the reflecting X-rays into the detector, and thus the low intensity of diffraction peaks. In addition, the crystallite size of PtNi and CA@PtNi hNS corresponding to the most prominent peak around 40.6° was calculated using Scherer's formula [26] and found to be 3.07 nm and 2.36 nm, respectively. The observed reduction in the crystallite size of CA@PtNi hNS was attributed to the high dispersion of the nanoparticles, which concurs with TEM data [27].

The structure and morphology of the prepared PtNi hNS and CA@PtNi hNS were further investigated using TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . TEM image [Fig. 2(a)] reveals that the as-synthesized PtNi hNS consist of irregular, aggregated nanostructures with an average diameter of 10.5 ± 3 nm [Fig. 2(a)]. An additional TEM image of PtNi revealing the existence of hollow nanostructures is shown in supplementary data [Fig. S2]. In contrast, TEM image [Fig. 2(b)] indicates that the CA@PtNi hNS consist of well-dispersed, hollow nanospheres with an average diameter of 10.3 ± 2 nm. The inset of Fig. 2 (a) & (b) shows the size distribution graph of the hollow nanospheres. This result suggests that the introduction of CA as a surfactant during nanoparticle growth results in highly-dispersed and uniform nanospheres. Fig. 2 (c) shows a high-resolution TEM image of a well-defined single CA@PtNi hollow nanosphere with a wall thickness of 2 nm. The lattice spacing values for the adjacent fringes of 0.20 nm and 0.22 nm correspond to the (200) and (111) planes of the PtNi structure, respectively. Moreover, indexed rings in selected area electron diffraction pattern [Fig. 2(d)] are ascribed to the (110), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the PtNi structure, which supports the XRD results.

A comparison of the FTIR spectra of CA@PtNi hNS and that of commercially available CA validated the presence of CA-related functional groups in the CA@PtNi hNS [Fig. 3 (a)]. FTIR spectra of commercially available CA reveal characteristic peaks at 1742 and 1693 cm−1, which correspond to carbonyl asymmetric stretching (C=O) in the COOH group. The peaks at 3491 and 3282 cm−1 are assigned to hydroxyl groups, a weak IR band observed in the range from 2850 to 3000 cm−1 is assigned to CH stretching of aliphatic compounds. The peak at 1134 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching associated with the tertiary alcohol group (C-OH), and the 771 cm−1 peak ascribed to CH2 groups [28–30]. The disappearance of peak related to CH2 group (771 cm−1) along with shifting of the FTIR peak linked to CO at 1636 cm−1 in the spectrum of CA@PtNi hNS indicates binding of CA to the surface of the metallic nanoparticles by chemical sorption of the carboxylate ions [31,32]. The interaction of CA molecules with Pt or Ni metallic surface via COOH groups results in a decrease in the intensity of peak correspond to CO stretching [33]. In this way, the relatively increased intensity of peak related to CH stretching is readily visible at 2982 cm−1 in the spectrum for CA@PtNi hNS than in the case of the spectrum for CA [34–37].

The influence of CA on the surface charge of the PtNi was determined using zeta potential measurements [Fig. 3 (b)). The value of the zeta potential for PtNi and CA@PtNi hNS in aqueous solution was found to be 23 mV and -11 mV, respectively. The negative surface potential of the CA@PtNi hNS is attributed to the presence of negatively charged functional groups associated with CA [38].

The composition of CA@PtNi hNS were investigated using XPS, as displayed in Fig. 4 . The XPS spectra related to Pt 4f, Ni 2p, C 1s, and O 1s were calibrated by the binding energy of C 1s at 284.6 eV [39]. Fig. 4(a) shows the combined high-resolution XPS spectra of Pt 4f and Ni 3p. Deconvolution of the spectra revealed peaks centered at 74.9 eV and 71.6 eV, which correspond to metallic Pt (Pt0) in the Pt 4f doublet (Pt 4f5/2, Pt 4f7/2), whereas peaks around 68.7 eV is attributed to Ni 3p. The deconvolution of Ni 2p spectra indicate doublet peaks at 869.0 eV (Ni 2p1/2) and 851.6 eV (Ni 2p3/2), which correspond to metallic Ni (Ni0) [Fig. 4(b)]. Here, a negative shift in peak related to Ni0 as compared with pure Ni (852.6 eV) could be attributed to the incorporation of Pt on the Ni surface (PtNi alloying) [40,41]. Except for these peaks, two additional peaks appeared at the higher binding energies of 857.3 eV, and 874.8 eV, which were noted as satellite peaks [41]. The elemental ratio (at.%) of Pt: Ni in the CA@ PtNi hNS was calculated to be 60: 40%. The higher amount of Pt in CA@PtRu indicates the existence of the Pt-rich hollow nanosphere surface. Also, the deconvolution of C 1s and O1s spectra further confirmed the existence of CA in the CA@PtNi hNS [Fig. 4 (c) and (d)]. The C 1s spectrum reveals three peaks centered at 288.3, 286.2, and 284.6 eV, which are linked to COOH, CO, and C-C/C=C, respectively. The O1s spectrum shows peaks at 534.7 and 531.1 eV, which correspond to C-O/C=O and OH, respectively.

Moreover, SEM-EDX mapping of CA@PtNi hNS powder sample was investigated to validate further the existence of Pt and Ni [Fig. S3]. Within a specific area (a) on CA@PtNi hNS sample, the EDX mapping shows uniform distribution of (b) Ni, (c) Pt, and (d) PtNi in purple, yellow, and purple-yellow color, respectively. The EDX result (e) estimated the atomic percentage (at.%) of Pt and Ni as 58.79% and 41.21%, which is entirely closed to the values determined using XPS (60 and 40%).

A progression of investigations was carried out to explore the enzyme-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS. The effect of reaction conditions, such as the pH of the phthalate buffer, the surrounding temperature, the nanozyme concentration, and the incubation time, were investigated using a time-dependent absorbance measurement [Fig. 5 ]. The most extreme reaction velocity for TMB oxidation in the presence of H2O2 was seen in pH 4.0 phthalate buffer, decreasing at more acidic, neutral, or basic pH conditions. This result demonstrates that the CA@PtNi hNS catalyze the oxidation of TMB under weakly acidic conditions. Moreover, the observed decrease in reaction velocity under neutral and basic pH conditions is attributed to the weak solubility of TMB [42]. Whereas under highly acidic conditions decrease in the formation of various hydration shell water in CA@PtNi hNS surface is ascribed to a decrease in reaction velocity [43,44].

The temperature-dependent peroxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS was examined at 10–70 °C using an optimized pH 4.0 phthalate buffer. Successive increases to the peroxidase-like activity were observed up until 30 °C; beyond this temperature, the activity decreased. This behavior could be explained by the increase in the number of catalytic sites on the CA@PtNi hNS with increases to temperature (up until 30 °C) that leads to an increase of peroxidase-like activity, while higher temperatures (above 30 °C) achieves an invalid breakdown of H2O2 that results in a decrease in activity [45]. Thus, 30 °C was considered the optimum temperature, which delivered the most energy for CA@PtNi hNS and substrate binding. However, for convenience, all experiments were performed at room temperature (25 °C). A linear relationship was observed between CA@ PtNi hNS concentration and their peroxidase-like activity. Moreover, a significant increase in absorbance was observed for the first 120 s, which then saturated. Thus, the incubation time for the reaction was considered to be 120 s.

Fig. 6 (a) displays time-dependent absorbance spectra for the oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activity of CA@ PtNi hNS, along with the control spectra (in the absence of CA@PtNi hNS). In the presence of H2O2, CA@PtNi hNS catalyze the oxidation of TMB substrate and produce a blue-colored product, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6(a). This result reveals the high peroxidase-like activity of CA@ PtNi hNS. In the absence of H2O2, the interaction between CA@PtNi hNS and TMB substrate produces a light blue product, which suggests the weak oxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS. This weak oxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS is negligible compared to its high peroxidase-like activity. Furthermore, a comparative study of the peroxidase activities of Ni, Pt, PtNi, and CA@PtNi hNS with a specific concentration of 20 μg mL−1 was carried out using time-dependent absorbance [Fig. 6(b)]. The as-synthesized Ni nanoparticles do not exhibit significant intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, whereas Pt nanoparticles reveal good peroxidase-like characteristics. As compared to individual Ni or Pt nanoparticles, incorporation of Pt with Ni (PtNi) demonstrated dramatically enhanced peroxidase-like activity due to the heterogeneous interaction between Pt and Ni atoms and formation of a hollow nanosphere with Pt rich surface. However, aggregation of PtNi hNS (as observed in TEM results) reduces the active surface area for peroxidase-like activity. The modification of PtNi hNS with CA results in further improved peroxidase-like activity. This improved peroxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS could be attributed to the heterogeneous interaction between atoms, the Pt-rich hollow nanosphere surface, large surface area, and negatively-charged surface that binds to TMB closed to the metallic surface.

To study the efficacy of CA@PtNi hNS in catalyzing the oxidation of H2O2 in the presence of TMB (0.5 mM), the time-reaction spectra at 652 nm was recorded for a range of H2O2 concentrations (0.01, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, and 3.5 mM), as shown in Fig. 7(a). The initial linear portion of the time-reaction curve (0–60 s) has a regression constant (R
2 ) of close to 1, so it was chosen for calculating the values of the K
m and V
max parameters [Fig. 7(b)] [46]. A typical Michaelis–Menten curve was obtained between reaction rate (v, μM s−1) and H2O2 concentration (mM) [Fig. 7(c)]. Finally, a Lineweaver–Burk curve [Fig. 7 (d)], i.e., the double reciprocal plot of Michaelis–Menten curve, was plotted to calculate kinetic parameters: K
m and V
max . The values of K
m and V
max for the catalysis of the oxidation of H2O2 by CA@PtNi hNS were estimated to be 2.34 mM and 36.75 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively, (Table 1 ). The lower value of K
m for CA@PtNi hNS compared to HRP3 and other nanozymes [3,47–55,57] suggests that it has a higher affinity to H2O2. The high surface to volume ratio and the functional groups linked to CA@PtNi hNS also contribute to catalyzing H2O2 oxidation. The higher value of V
max compared to HRP [3] and other studied nanozymes [3,47–55,57] indicates the ability of CA@PtNi hNS to catalyze a larger amount (36.75 × 10−8 M) of H2O2 per second.

A similar procedure was employed to study the effectiveness of CA@PtNi hNS to catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 (2 mM). A series of time-dependent absorbance spectra was recorded with various TMB concentrations (0.02, 0.06, 0.12, 0.18, 0.24, 0.30, 0.36, 0.42 mM) in ethanol [Fig. S4 (a)]. The first 60 s of the initial linear portion (R
2 ≈ 1) of the reaction-time curve was chosen for the analysis of enzyme-like kinetics [Fig. S4 (b)] [46]. A typical Michaelis–Menten curve, the curve between reaction rate (v, μM s−1) and TMB substrate concentrations (C), and was obtained, as shown in Fig. S4 (c). Further, a Lineweaver–Burk plot (1/v versus 1/C) was constructed and analyzed for the kinetic parameters K
m and V
max [Fig. S4 (d)]. The values of K
m and V
max for the catalysis of the oxidation of TMB by CA@PtNi hNS were calculated to be 0.18 mM and 21.89 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively (Table 1). In this case, the CA@PtNi hNS yielded a lower value of K
m compared to horseradish peroxidase (HRP)3 and other nanozymes [3,47–57], indicating its higher affinity to TMB substrate. The presence of functional groups on the CA@PtNi hNS from the CA provides an excellent environment for binding the TMB substrate closed to PtNi surface. Moreover, the high surface to volume ratio of the CA@PtNi hNS contributes to catalyzing the reaction. The K
m value also suggests that 0.18 mM TMB is the optimal concentration for occupying half of the active sites on the CA@PtNi hNS. The value of V
max for the CA@PtNi hNS is the highest among HRP [3] and other mentioned nanozymes [3,47–57], suggesting that CA@PtNi hNS can catalyze the maximum number of TMB molecules per second.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of anti-HSA and HSA with CA@PtNi hNS
ce:section.ce:para: The CA@PtNi hNS were conjugated to anti-HSA for the specific detection of HSA. Conjugation was confirmed by comparing the FTIR spectra of unmodified and modified CA@PtNi hNS with spectra of anti-HSA [Fig. S5]. The FTIR spectrum of anti-HSA (curve a), reveals peaks centred at 1636 and 1543 cm−1, which correspond to peptide groups of protein (CO stretching and NH deformation). On the other hand, the FTIR spectrum of CA@PtNi hNS (curve b) possesses a strong peak at 1634 cm−1 due to the presence of CA linked CO groups. After CA@PtNi hNS functionalization with anti-HSA, a shift in the peak related to CO (from 1634 cm−1 to 1615 cm−1) was observed along with the appearance of new peaks around 1543 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 that are linked to NH and amide II band, respectively [59]. The existence of an amide-II band at 1515 cm−1 suggests chemical interaction between the COOH of CA@PtNi hNS and the NH2 of anti-HSA. The CA@PtNi hNS – anti-HSA conjugation also occurs via strong physical interaction of anti-HSA COOH groups with uncovered Pt rich surface of nanospheres [33]. The both type of interactions make a stable adsorption anti-HSA on the CA@PtNi hNS surface. Also, a decrease (~20%) in the peroxidase-like activity of the CA@PtNi hNS was observed after anti-HSA functionalization, as shown in Fig. S6 (a). The above results suggest successful conjugation of anti-HSA to CA@PtNi hNS.

Besides this, the difference in the peroxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi before and after the treatment with HSA antigen was observed to check the possibility of interaction between HSA and CA@PtNi hNS [Fig. S6 (b)]. In this process, 100 μL CA@PtNi hNS (30 μg mL−1) and 100 μL HSA (50 ng mL−1) in PBS were mixed in the Eppendorf tube followed by incubation on the shaker for 1 h. Subsequently, the obtained product was washed with PBS to discard unattached HSA, and its peroxidase-like activity was measured in phthalate buffer (pH 4) solution containing 2 mM H2O2 and 0.5 mM TMB. The obtained negligible change in the peroxidase-like activity of CA@PtNi hNS before and after the treatment with HSA antigen, suggest higher stability of CA@PtNi hNS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of HSA by CA@PtNi hNS
ce:section.ce:para:
The CA@PtNi hNS is employed as a peroxidase mimic to detect HSA concentration using a colorimetric technique. A schematic representation of ELISA and NLISA for the detection of HSA is shown in Fig. 8(a). The NLISA is developed by replacing the enzyme in the ELISA kit for HSA with CA@PtNi hNS. During the measurement, the wells of each column of a 96-well plate (pre-coated with anti-HSA capture antibody) are treated with different concentrations of HSA (0–400 ng mL−1). In this way, each well of the column possesses an affinity for binding different concentrations of anti-HSA detection antibodies or anti-HSA–CA@PtNi hNS conjugates. On addition of working solutions to each well containing different concentrations of anti-HSA–CA@PtNi hNS conjugates result in blue color products (oxidized TMB) with different intensities. Since CA@PtNi hNS activity depends linearly on concentration, the intensity of the blue color in each well depends on the concentration of attached anti-HSA-CA@PtNi hNS conjugates in that well. However, the blue color of the product changed to yellow upon adding the stop solution to the wells. The schematic for color change (blue or yellow) and change in color intensity of wells for various concentrations of HSA after the reaction with working and stop solutions are shown in Fig. S7. Finally, the absorbance of each well at 450 nm was measured, and the results plotted against the utilized HSA concentration [Fig. 8(b)]. The inset of Fig. 8(b) shows the optical intensity of wells after adding the stop solution for ELISA and NLISA methods. The developed NLISA assay detected HSA in the linear range of 0–400 ng mL−1 with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.19 ng mL−1 and R2 of 0.97. In contrast, traditional ELISA revealed a similar linear detection range with a LOD of 0.15 ng mL−1, and R
2 of 0.96. The LOD was calculated using formula 3 σ/m, where m is the slope of the calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal. When comparing the slope values of the linear curves, it observed that NLISA is 120% more sensitive than ELISA; however, ELISA has a 21% lower LOD than NLISA.

Furthermore, the long-term stability of the CA@PtNi hNS was investigated. The peroxidase-like activity of the CA@PtNi hNS (powder) was interrogated for a month at one-week intervals [Fig. S8]. After the completion of one month, the CA@PtNi hNS retained about 80% of their peroxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery test of HSA from spiked human plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of CA@PtNi hNS-based NLISA to recover HSA from human plasma samples is investigated and compared with standard ELISA. Table 2 displays the percentage recovery of HSA using the ELISA and NLISA techniques with corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) value. The observed the high value of percentage recovery could be ascribed to the pre-exist HSA in human serum plasma itself. As shown in Table 2, the values of relative percent difference (RPD) for ELISA and NLISA to detect 50 ng mL−1 and 200 ng mL−1 concentrations of HSA spiked samples was observed to be 3.75% and 4.23%, respectively. These measured values of relative percentage difference (RPD) are quite good and suggest that the developed CA@PtNi hNS-based NLISA can be applied to detect HSA in biological samples.

In summary, bimetallic CA@PtNi hNS synthesized through a one-pot wet chemical method demonstrates surprisingly improved peroxidase-like activity due to a Pt-rich surface, heterogeneous interaction between Pt and Ni atoms, and large surface area. The use of CA during synthesis not only stabilized the CA@PtNi hNS but also offered good dispersion to nanostructures and altered the surface charge from positive to negative that helped to bind TMB substrate. As a peroxidase mimic, CA@PtNi hNS follows typical Michaeli-Menten kinetics and their catalytic activity was dependent on temperature, pH, catalyst concentration, and concentration of H2O2 or TMB substrate. The proposed methodology reveals an excellent approach for designing new nanocatalysts with improved activity. As a peroxidase mimic, CA@PtNi hNS based-NLISA showed sensitive detection of HSA for concentrations ranging from 0 to 400 ng mL−1 with a LOD of 0.19 ng mL−1. Moreover, developed CA@PtNi hNS based-NLISA able to recover HSA from the spiked human plasma samples. With the advantages of facile preparation, high stability, and peroxidase-like activity, the CA@PtNi hNS may be well-suited to other applications in the fields of environmental and biomedical sciences. However, in the NLISA method, sometimes we may face issues related to limited substrate selectivity, ambiguous mechanism of signal amplification, nanozyme morphology, and nano-toxicity.
